_meta.issue: #124 [M16] Formative path S4 — AI agents (training module, tune interaction)
_meta.interaction: Agent parameter tuning (kind: tune) — formative exercise, NOT scored toward completion
_meta.source: Holak Scale maturity axes: output verifiability, decision reversibility, control of agents. Attribution: quality-blog.eu/pl/blog/skala-holaka.
_meta.dataPolicy: ALL examples SYNTHETIC and generic (no real data/PII). Formative module — no pass threshold, no certificate.

intro: An agent is not a chatbot: it executes steps and uses tools, so it can change the real world — files, emails, data, a repository. Mature work with an agent means matching autonomy to risk: the harder the effect is to undo and the output to verify, the narrower the permissions and the more human control. This module teaches you to tune those levers. It is a growth exercise, not a test.

blocks.text: A chatbot answers with text. A workflow with a tool runs one defined step. An agent plans and executes many steps, reaching for tools on its own — which is exactly why it needs boundaries. The question is not „can the agent do it”, but „what happens if it gets it wrong, and can we undo it in time”.

items: Workflow with a tool — executes one defined step.
Agent — plans and executes many steps with access to tools.

blocks.title: A Holak Scale axis
blocks.text: Designing work so AI-supported decisions (including agent actions) stay checkable and reversible is a mark of reaching a higher level.

Agent safety is not about trust but about setting three levers: the permission scope (what the agent can do at all), the human approval point (when it must ask), and the audit trail and reversibility (whether you can check and undo what it did).

items: Permission scope — from read-only to full production write.
Human-in-the-loop — approval always, only for irreversible actions, or never.
Trail and reversibility — full log with rollback, basic log, or none.

blocks.title: The „the agent will manage” trap
blocks.text: The wider the access and the less control, the cheaper… until the first irreversible error. By default narrow the permissions and require approval where the effect is hard to undo.

Below you'll set three agent parameters and see the risk profile change live. Then in the checkpoint you'll pick a safe set for a task with irreversible effects. No pass threshold — the point is the reflex of matching autonomy to risk.

interaction.title: Tune agent autonomy and control
interaction.intro: Set the three levers and watch the risk profile. Then answer the checkpoint — this is a growth exercise, not a test.

levels.label: Read-only
levels.effect: The agent changes nothing — lowest risk, good for analysis and proposals.

levels.label: Sandbox write
levels.effect: Changes only in an isolated environment — easy to discard.

levels.label: Full production write
levels.effect: The agent can permanently change production data — highest risk, needs control.

levels.label: Always before acting
levels.effect: A human approves every step — slower, but fully controlled.

levels.label: For irreversible decisions
levels.effect: Approval required when the effect cannot be undone — a sensible default compromise.

levels.label: Never (full autonomy)
levels.effect: The agent acts without asking — fast, but an error may go unnoticed.

levels.label: Full log + rollback
levels.effect: Every action recorded and reversible — full checkability.

levels.label: Basic log
levels.effect: You know what happened, but undoing is often manual and hard.

levels.label: No trail
levels.effect: You can't tell what the agent did or why — no checkability.

checkpoint.prompt: The agent is to help with a task that may PERMANENTLY change production data (an effect hard to undo). Which set of settings is the safest to start with?

options.text: Full production write, never approve, no trail — „the agent will manage”.
Read-only or sandbox, human approval for irreversible actions, full log with rollback.
Full production write, basic log, no approval — „let's go fast, check later”.

checkpoint.feedbackCorrect: Right. For irreversible effects you narrow permissions, require human approval, and ensure a full trail you can roll back.
checkpoint.feedbackIncorrect: For irreversible effects, autonomy without control and a trail is asking for an irreversible error. Narrow the permissions, add human approval, and a full log with rollback.

points: An agent can change the real world — match autonomy to the task's risk and reversibility.
Three control levers: permission scope, human approval, audit trail with rollback.
By default narrow permissions; the harder the effect is to undo, the more human control.
checklist: Do I know the worst the agent could do with its current permissions?
Do irreversible actions require human approval?
Is an audit trail kept, and can the agent's action be undone?
summary.checklistTitle: Checklist — before you let an agent in
summary.nextStep: Next step: for one real agent task, write down the worst possible effect and match the permission scope, approval point, and audit trail to it.
